Which one is for you?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorOver the last two years, there has been a huge shift in the AI UI generation space. What was once a tool that only gave us basic mockups has now evolved into a fully fledged code environment.
Seeing this, I wondered, what if I test 5 of the most popular AI UI generators by giving them the same prompt?
By analyzing each one’s strengths and weaknesses, we can determine which one is best suited for which situation and actually provide value.
Here is the prompt I am going to be using for all the tools:
“Design a dark-mode, professional ‘FIFA World Cup Analytics Dashboard’ for a sports data analyst. The design must be clean, data-heavy, and use modern Tailwind spacing and typography. Generate three connected screens: 1. The Main Live Dashboard (KPI cards, live action log). 2. Player Predictions (AI Agent Chat view). 3. Team Stats Table.”
Let's see how each one handled the task.

1) Flowstep
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorOverview:
Flowstep focuses on the whole end-to-end user journey rather than single pages, structuring three distinct panels that feel like a single, smooth app environment.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorPress enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorPress enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorHow It Handled The Prompt
Flowstep followed the prompt extremely well. It mapped out all three requested views side by side using a uniform, dark-mode aesthetic. The data density is high, using realistic football datasets such as match logs rather than generic text placeholders.
The Good
The layout consistency across screens is top-notch. The left navigation bar stays perfectly in place across views, and the line graph tracking match momentum looks highly polished and perfect for a data analyst.
It also exports React, TypeScript, and Tailwind code and can paste designs directly into Figma. This makes it easy to take a multi-screen concept into a real design and development workflow.
The Bad
The overall design is well organized, but some parts of the interface feel a little rigid. The typography in the stats table is text-heavy to read. The chat interface also relies on bright green message boxes, which don’t feel as polished or premium as the rest of the design.
Best For
Flowstep is perfect if you need to show how a user moves through many screens without the design falling apart.

2) Google Stitch (Formerly Galileo AI)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorOverview
Next up is Google Stitch. Google Stitch is regarded as one of the leading AI UI generators for creating polished, high-quality interface concepts directly from a text prompt.
Source: Image By AuthorSource: Image By AuthorSource: Image By AuthorHow It Handled The Prompt
Stitch handled the sports analyst prompt very well. It generated three clean, interconnected screens: a live match center with a pitch heatmap, a sophisticated predictive chat screen, and a dense tournament table.
The Good
The visual fidelity and attention to detail here are really good. The dashboard includes helpful extra indicators, such as data sources and live speed trackers, and uses bold fonts for headings and sharp, clear numbers.
The Bad
The heavy focus on data relies a bit too much on pure visual style. The massive green headers look beautiful but take up a lot of screen space and are even cut off at the top. That space could have been used to show more rows in the stats table.
Best For
Google Stitch is an excellent choice when you need to create visuals to present to stakeholders or clients and get your design approved.

3) Figma Make (Figma AI)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorOverview
Now let’s look at Figma Make. This isn’t a separate website but a tool built directly into Figma, which most designers already use for work.
It focuses on making organized, professional-looking dashboards for businesses.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorPress enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorPress enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorHow It Handled The Prompt
Figma Make handled the multi-screen requirements really well. The Main Dashboard features include a cumulative timeline graph and a performance radar chart, while the stats table maps out standard league tracking metrics.
The Good
The addition of specialized dashboard widgets is exceptional. The inclusion of a radar chart and detailed form guides shows the engine understands sports data. Because it builds natively inside Figma, the layout relies on clean, easily editable grid structures.
The Bad
The style feels a bit safe and boring. It uses standard business designs and plain blue backgrounds. If you want something unique, you’ll have to change it yourself.
Best For
Figma Make is the go-to tool for designers who want a solid starting point that they can easily edit inside their favorite workspace.

4) V0 (by Vercel)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorOverview:
Now we go with v0. If you are a front-end developer, v0 needs absolutely no introduction. It completely flipped the AI UI space by throwing away the traditional design canvas. Instead, it creates ready-to-use code components.
Let’s see how it handled the benchmark parameters:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorPress enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorPress enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorHow It Handled The Prompt
Because v0 writes code, it was great at following the specific technical instructions. It built a very clean, simple, dark design that looks very professional.
The Good
The data presentation feels highly natural and professional. Attacking momentum meters, side-panel indicators, and accurate filtering tags show immense structural intelligence. The responsive alignment across columns is just too good.
The Bad
It sticks strictly to standard UI code blocks and basic component libraries, meaning it won’t give you artistic graphic depth or illustrative designs without massive text guiding.
Best For
v0 is great if you are a front-end developer who wants to skip the initial structural coding phase and immediately copy-paste clean, functional React components directly into a code editor.

5) Lovable
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorOverview
Finally, let’s talk about Lovable. If you’ve been tracking full-stack AI development engines, you know Lovable represents a massive shift. It acts as a full-stack engine designed to build entire, living web applications from scratch through pure conversation.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorPress enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorPress enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Image By AuthorHow It Handled The Prompt
Lovable delivered a smooth application flow. It built three integrated panels: a live overview, a layered prediction workspace, and a sorted team statistics table.
The Good
The way it thinks is elite. It included extra tools on the screen that felt ready to use with real data, making the whole thing feel very finished.
The Bad
Because it tries to build a full app, it sometimes uses very basic designs. Many parts look like standard templates unless you tell it to make them more interesting.
Best For
Lovable is amazing if you need to rapidly transition from a text prompt to a multi-screen web application prototype that handles user input and real-world data workflows.

Conclusion
The field of AI UI generation is evolving rapidly. It is completely understandable to feel overwhelmed by the number of new design tools and subscription models launching every single week.
However, knowing how these tools work can help you work much faster. These tests show that the gap between designing and coding is almost gone, but no single tool is perfect for everyone.
If your goal is to design a complete user journey across multiple screens, Flowstep is the strongest choice. It does an excellent job of keeping the entire product consistent across screens.
If you need beautiful, high-fidelity designs to quickly impress clients or stakeholders, Google Stitch stands out with its polished visuals.
If you already work inside Figma and want a well-organized starting point that you can easily edit and refine, Figma Make is the best option.
If you’re a front-end developer who wants clean React code to start a project, v0 is the best option. It generates components that are easy to build on.
Finally, if you’re a founder looking to take an idea from concept to a working multi-screen web application as quickly as possible, Lovable is an excellent choice.
